rawContent:
Tweet by @gabrielchua (Gabriel Chua)
Author bio: DX Engineer, Codex @OpenAI || Opinions my own
Text: https://t.co/xGBxV5ZTzr
Created: 2026-06-17T15:05:21.000Z
Metrics: {"retweet_count":26,"reply_count":11,"like_count":217,"quote_count":11,"bookmark_count":648,"impression_count":153248}
Article content: {"preview_text":"Every two hours, Codex prepares email replies for me to review.
Most of the drafts are good. Yet I still change them.
I might add a decision from an older thread or an in-person conversation, soften","media_entities":["3_2067142568941658112"],"cover_media":"3_2067140860882616320","plain_text":"Every two hours, Codex prepares email replies for me to review.
Most of the drafts are good. Yet I still change them.
I might add a decision from an older thread or an in-person conversation, soften the tone because I know the sender, or remove a commitment I'm not ready to make. The draft can be well written and still feel generic. That generic feeling is often called "AI slop," but in cases like this, the problem isn't intelligence. It's missing context.
No one did anything wrong. Codex worked from the information it had, while my edits supplied context it didn't. What's interesting is that those edits contain context too. They reveal what mattered in this situation: what I kept, what I changed, what I rejected, and what I wasn't comfortable sending.
Most automations don't preserve that context. The next run starts from the same incomplete picture.
I find it useful to think about context in two categories.
First, there's the context needed before the work begins: history, facts, constraints, relationships, and prior decisions.
Second, there's the context revealed after the work: what a person keeps, changes, rejects, sends, or leaves pending during review.
That gives us two loops. The inner loop brings the right context to the task and produces a draft. The outer loop learns from review and makes that context available the next time the work runs.
The Inner Loop Brings Context to the Work
For email, the inner loop decides whether a message needs a reply, finds relevant context, drafts a response, checks its claims, and creates a draft for review.
The workflow can be fixed in a workflow written in code or built with a workflow builder: fetch messages, filter noise, classify, draft, and check the result. Or Codex can decide what steps to take from a simple prompt like “Create drafts every morning at 9am for emails I need to reply”, and it’ll inspect new threads, recover relevant history, research what is missing, prepare replies, and surface anything unclear.
Either approach can work. The important part is that retrieval is part of writing.
A reply may depend on a similar email, a six-month-old decision, the current status in a project tracker, or a fact from an approved source. Codex can gather that context or ask a subagent to research a specific question. The goal is not to retrieve everything, but to find the smallest set of information that makes the reply accurate and specific.
I keep the action reversible: create the draft, never send it. Before I edit, the inner loop saves the proposed reply, its sources, and the versions of its prompt and writing guidance. Without that record, review is an anecdote. With it, review becomes evidence.
The Outer Loop Recovers Context From Review
The outer loop starts after I review the draft. Was it sent unchanged, edited and sent, deleted, or left pending?
Those results mean different things. An unchanged send suggests the draft worked. An edited send gives a before-and-after pair. A deleted draft is harder to read: perhaps it was off, or perhaps no reply was needed. A pending draft tells us little.
Even the sent email records only what I accepted, not whether the reply worked. The real result may appear in the response or work that follows. Still, review adds evidence that was not available when the draft was written.
The difference between the draft and the sent version is evidence. It is not automatically a lesson, and it should not automatically become a prompt change.
A shorter opening may show a writing preference. An added fact may reveal that the inner loop searched the wrong place. A removed commitment may point to a check the workflow needs. A rewritten paragraph may reflect judgment that should remain human.
A diff can show what changed. Understanding what it means is the real work of the outer loop.
The Outer Loop Improves the Inner Loop
The outer loop exists to make the next inner-loop run better.
It reviews what changed between Codex’s draft and the version I sent, then asks: what would have helped Codex get closer the first time?
The answer may be better writing guidance, another source, a new search step, a check for unsupported commitments, or an earlier handoff to me. Not every edit becomes a rule. The outer loop looks for patterns, proposes the smallest useful change, and I decide what to keep.
In practice, I would keep these approved lessons in a simple markdown file. The outer loop proposes updates to that file; the inner automation reads it before drafting the next email.
That is how review improves the inner loop: what I correct today becomes useful context for tomorrow’s run.
Two Loops, Two Clocks
 
The loops run at different speeds. The inner loop helps quickly, perhaps every two hours. The outer loop waits for enough examples, running at day’s end, after ten reviewed drafts, or weekly when examples are sparse.
Run the outer loop too often and it may learn from an unusual case. Never run it and useful corrections disappear with the next draft.
The same pattern applies to decks, reports, briefs, and issue triage. In each case, the work improves when the workflow can use both kinds of context: what was known before the first draft and what review revealed afterward.
Models are already capable. The opportunity is to build workflows that do not make them rediscover the same context on every run.
The inner loop brings the right context to the work. The outer loop preserves the context revealed by the work.","title":"The Inner and Outer Loops of Codex Automations "}
